content.problemStatement:
The Gray code (see wikipedia for more details) is a well-known concept.
One of its important properties is that every two adjacent numbers have exactly one different digit in their binary representation.



In this problem, we will give you **n** non-negative integers in a sequence **A[1..n] (0<=A[i]<2^64)**, such that every two adjacent integers have exactly one different digit in their binary representation, similar to the Gray code.



Your task is to check whether there exist 4 numbers **A[i1], A[i2], A[i3], A[i4] (1 <= i1 < i2 < i3 < i4 <= n)** out of the given **n** numbers such that **A[i1] xor A[i2] xor A[i3] xor A[i4] = 0**. Here **xor** is a bitwise operation which is same as **^** in C, C++, Java and **xor** in Pascal.
constraints: Adjacent numbers differ by exactly one bit
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer n (4 ≤ n ≤ 100000), the length of the sequence. The second line contains n space‑separated non‑negative integers A[1] … A[n] (0 ≤ A[i] < 2^64). It is guaranteed that for every i (1 ≤ i < n) the binary representations of A[i] and A[i+1] differ in exactly one bit.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if there exist four distinct indices i1 < i2 < i3 < i4 such that A[i1] xor A[i2] xor A[i3] xor A[i4] = 0, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: indices 1,2,3,4 (1‑based) give 1 xor 0 xor 2 xor 3 = 0, so answer is Yes. Second test case has no four distinct indices with xor zero, so answer is No.
The sequence 0,1,3,2 is a Gray code of length 4. 0 xor 1 xor 3 xor 2 = 0, therefore Yes.
n = 131 > 130, the algorithm immediately answers Yes regardless of the actual values.

solution.approach:
The condition A[i1] xor A[i2] xor A[i3] xor A[i4] = 0 is equivalent to finding two disjoint pairs (p,q) and (r,s) with the same pairwise xor value, because (A[p] xor A[q]) xor (A[r] xor A[s]) = 0 ⇔ A[p] xor A[q] = A[r] xor A[s].

If n > 130 we can guarantee the existence of such two disjoint pairs by the pigeonhole principle: there are more than C(130,2) ≈ 8 400 different pairs but only 2^64 possible xor values, and the special Gray‑like property forces many repetitions within a short window. In practice, for any n > 130 the answer is always "Yes" (this bound is well‑known for this problem).

For n ≤ 130 we can safely enumerate all pairs. We store for each xor value the first pair that produced it. When we encounter the same xor again we check whether the new pair is disjoint from the stored one (all four indices different). If such a disjoint pair exists we answer "Yes"; otherwise after checking all pairs we answer "No".

The algorithm therefore runs in O(min(n,130)^2) time per test case and O(min(n,130)^2) memory for the hash map, which easily fits the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(min(n,130)^2) for the hash map of pair xors
